name: Type 26 City-class frigate
overview_md:
The Type 26 City-class frigate represents the Royal Navy's most advanced anti-submarine warfare platform and the cornerstone of Britain's future surface fleet. Designed as the replacement for the aging Type 23 Duke-class frigates, the Type 26 embodies a radical shift toward modular, adaptable naval architecture optimized for high-end ASW operations in contested environments. The program, initiated in the early 2010s, reflects the UK's recognition that traditional frigate designs were inadequate for facing modern submarine threats from near-peer adversaries.

Strategically, the Type 26 serves as the Royal Navy's primary ASW hunter-killer, designed to operate independently or as part of carrier strike groups. Its design philosophy prioritizes acoustic stealth, advanced sonar capabilities, and mission flexibility through modular payload bays. The platform's sophisticated hull form, derived from extensive computational fluid dynamics modeling, achieves unprecedented noise reduction for a surface combatant—critical for detecting modern diesel-electric and nuclear submarines.

What sets the Type 26 apart from its predecessors is its integrated approach to ASW warfare. The combination of the Type 2087 towed array sonar, hull-mounted Type 2050 sonar, and advanced acoustic signature management creates what BAE Systems describes as 'the world's most capable ASW frigate.' The ship's modular mission bay allows for rapid reconfiguration between ASW, special forces operations, humanitarian missions, or mine countermeasures—addressing the Royal Navy's need for platform flexibility amid shrinking fleet numbers.

In the current threat environment, the Type 26 addresses a critical capability gap. Russian submarine activity has increased dramatically since 2014, with Kilo-class and improved Akula-class submarines operating closer to NATO shipping lanes. The Type 26's advanced sonar suite and Merlin helicopter integration provide the Royal Navy with its first purpose-built platform capable of detecting and engaging these threats at extended ranges. However, the program's troubled procurement history—marked by delays, cost overruns, and reduced orders from 13 to 8 hulls—highlights the broader challenges facing UK naval shipbuilding and raises questions about industrial capacity for sustained production.

specs_json.propulsion: Combined diesel-electric and gas turbine (CODLOG) - 1x MT30 gas turbine, 4x MTU diesel generators
specs_json.sonar: Ultra Electronics Type 2087 towed array, Type 2050 hull-mounted

additional.flight_deck: Large flight deck for Merlin or two Wildcat helicopters

armament_json.name: Strike Length VLS cells
armament_json.notes: For Tomahawk, LRASM, or future weapons (unfunded)

variants_json.name: Batch 1
variants_json.key_changes: Initial production variant with ARTISAN radar, basic CMS-1, no Strike Length VLS fitted

variants_json.name: Batch 2 (Proposed)
variants_json.key_changes: Potential upgrades including Type 4XX radar, enhanced electronic warfare, Strike Length VLS integration

news.title: BAE Systems and BMT win support deal for RAN’s Anzac-class frigates
news.snippet:
BAE Systems Australia – Maritime and BMT have secured the Anzac-class Designer Support Contract to provide support for the Royal Australian Navy’s Anzac-class frigates.
The post BAE Systems and BMT win support deal for RAN’s Anzac-class frigates appeared first on Naval Technology.

news.title: BAE Systems secures $146m contract for US Army M776 cannons
news.snippet: BAE Systems has received a contract to begin manufacturing M776 cannons, the primary armament for the US Army’s M777 towed 155mm howitzer.

news.title: BAE Systems tests APKWS laser-guidance kit on RAF Typhoon aircraft
news.snippet: BAE Systems has confirmed the successful test firing of the APKWS laser-guidance kit from a Eurofighter Typhoon aircraft.

news.title: BAE Systems trials low-cost counter drone solution for Eurofighter Typhoon
news.snippet: A BAE spokesperson told Breaking Defense that the air-to-surface test was internally funded, “as part of our commitment to invest in the advanced capabilities our customers require.”

news.title: BAE Systems secures $180m contract for TRIDON Mk2 from FMV
news.snippet: BAE Systems has signed a $180m worth contract with the Swedish Defence Materiel Administration (FMV) for the TRIDON Mk2 anti-aircraft system.

combat_history.event: No combat deployments - first ship HMS Glasgow not yet commissioned
combat_history.significance: Platform remains untested in operational environments

modernization.description: Integration of 24-cell Strike Length VLS for Tomahawk cruise missiles and future long-range weapons. Currently planned but unfunded.
modernization.impact: Would transform platform from primarily defensive ASW frigate to multi-mission surface combatant with significant strike capability

modernization.description: Potential replacement of ARTISAN radar with next-generation fixed-array system for enhanced air defense and ballistic missile tracking.
modernization.impact: Would significantly improve air defense capabilities and enable integration with future hypersonic threats

modernization.description: Integration of Ultra Electronics Series 2500 EW system and advanced communications intelligence capabilities.
modernization.impact: Enhanced survivability and intelligence gathering in contested electromagnetic environments

vulnerabilities.description: Limited medium-range air defense with only Sea Ceptor missiles and no long-range SAM capability. ARTISAN radar has limited performance against stealth targets and saturation attacks.
vulnerabilities.context: Vulnerable to coordinated air attacks from advanced adversaries with standoff weapons like Kh-35 or future hypersonic missiles
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Relies on escort vessels or carrier air wing for area air defense; future radar upgrades planned but unfunded

vulnerabilities.description: Initial batch lacks Strike Length VLS, limiting offensive capability to 127mm gun and helicopter-launched missiles
vulnerabilities.context: Cannot independently engage high-value targets or conduct land attack missions without VLS upgrade
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Strike Length VLS integration planned but requires additional funding and may not be retrofitted to early hulls

vulnerabilities.description: Highly complex platform with extensive reliance on international suppliers and BAE Systems' constrained shipbuilding capacity
vulnerabilities.context: Production delays and cost overruns highlight vulnerability of single-source procurement for critical components
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Limited by UK's reduced industrial base; some components sourced internationally but creates supply chain vulnerabilities

vulnerabilities.description: Reduced crew size of 157 creates sustainability challenges during extended operations and damage control scenarios
vulnerabilities.context: Royal Navy's manning crisis exacerbated by highly automated systems requiring specialized technical training
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Advanced automation and shore-based maintenance support, but remains vulnerable to crew fatigue on extended deployments

operational_patterns.typical_deployment: ASW patrol in GIUK Gap, CSG escort duty, or independent patrol in areas of interest
operational_patterns.typical_task_group: Operates with Type 45 destroyers in CSG or independently with RFA support
operational_patterns.readiness_notes: Not yet operational - first ship HMS Glasgow expected to achieve IOC in 2028-2029

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 26 prioritizes ASW over multi-mission capability, has superior acoustic signature management but less VLS capacity. Constellation-class emphasizes interoperability and cost control.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Anglo-American approaches to next-generation frigate design - specialization vs. flexibility

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 26 incorporates lessons from FREMM's modular design but emphasizes ASW specialization over FREMM's balanced multi-mission approach. Superior sonar suite but higher unit cost.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: European frigate evolution - how Type 26 learned from FREMM's successes and limitations

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 054A emphasizes numbers and cost efficiency over individual platform capability. Type 26 has superior ASW and acoustic performance but significantly higher unit cost limits procurement.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Quality vs quantity in naval procurement - Western high-end platforms vs Chinese mass production

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Gorshkov-class emphasizes strike warfare with Kalibr missiles while Type 26 prioritizes ASW. Both represent national naval resurgence but different tactical philosophies.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: ASW hunter vs strike platform - how different threat perceptions drive frigate design

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Hunter-class based on Type 26 hull but with American Aegis combat system and different sensor fit. Demonstrates adaptability of Type 26 design to different operational requirements.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: International collaboration in warship design - how allies adapt common platforms for national needs

images.description: Silhouettes of all planned future ships of the Royal Navy from the end of 2024

doctrine.role: The Type 26 serves as the Royal Navy's primary hunter-killer for peer adversary submarines, designed to operate independently or lead task groups in establishing undersea dominance in critical maritime corridors.
doctrine.employment: Typically deployed as the ASW anchor of Carrier Strike Groups or operating independently in forward patrol areas like the GIUK Gap. Forms the core of ASW task groups with Type 31 frigates providing area air defence and logistical support. Designed for sustained independent operations lasting 60+ days, with command facilities to coordinate multi-national ASW task forces. Expected to operate in high-threat environments where older frigates would require significant escort protection.
doctrine.design_philosophy: Designers prioritised acoustic stealth and ASW sensor performance above all else, accepting reduced magazine capacity and higher unit costs to achieve submarine-quiet signatures. The modular mission bay concept sacrifices some structural efficiency for operational flexibility, while the sophisticated integrated mast trades maintenance simplicity for reduced radar cross-section and electromagnetic compatibility.
doctrine.threat_context: Originally conceived to counter increasingly capable Russian nuclear submarines in North Atlantic chokepoints, particularly improved Akula and Yasen-class boats. The threat has evolved to include Chinese Type 095 submarines operating globally and advanced air-independent propulsion boats from multiple adversaries, requiring the Type 26's multi-frequency sonar suite and enhanced signal processing capabilities.
doctrine.comparison_frame: Compare primarily on acoustic signature levels and sonar processing power rather than traditional metrics like missile loadout or speed - the Type 26 trades weapons capacity for stealth. Towed array length, hull-mounted sonar frequency ranges, and mission bay flexibility are the key differentiators versus international ASW frigate designs. Cost-per-unit matters less than capability density given the small production run.

curated_sources.relevance: Friedman provides essential context on Royal Navy surface combatant evolution and the doctrinal drivers behind the Type 26 design requirements.
